Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 44 of about 109 target actions so far.

sections.content:
The board didn't change (board_changed: false). The green frame can't move LEFT further. It's blocked by the purple bar.
Let me reconsider the problem entirely. Maybe the goal in level 2 is NOT to move green frames into dark frames in the left quadrants. Maybe the goal is something else.
Let me look at the level 1 solution again. In level 1, I moved green frames into dark frames, and the level was completed. In level 2, I've moved 2 green frames into 2 dark frames in the bottom-right quadrant, but the level is not completed.
Wait, maybe the 2 green frames that are already inside dark frames in the bottom-right quadrant were NOT placed there by me. Maybe they were already there at the start of level 2. Let me check the history to see the initial state of level 2.
Actually, I don't have access to the initial state of level 2 anymore. But I can check the history to see when the green frames were placed inside the dark frames.
Let me think about this differently. In level 2, there are 4 green frames and 4 dark frames (2 in the bottom-right quadrant and 2 in the left quadrants). Two green frames are already inside dark frames in the bottom-right quadrant, and two green frames are outside dark frames in the bottom-right quadrant.
If the goal is to have all green frames inside dark frames, then the 2 green frames outside dark frames need to be moved into the 2 dark frames in the left quadrants. But the green frames can't reach the left quadrants.
Unless... the dark frames in the bottom-right quadrant are NOT targets. Maybe they are obstacles or decoys. And the 2 green frames inside them need to be moved OUT and then into the dark frames in the left quadrants.
But the green frames inside the dark frames have charcoal (c) centers, which means they're "completed" or "locked". I can't move them.
Hmm, let me try clicking on the green frame inside the dark frame to see if I can activate it.
Actually, wait. Let me look at the image again. The green frame id=9 (inside dark frame id=10) has a charcoal center. The green frame id=18 (inside dark frame id=17) has a charcoal center. These green frames are "completed" and can't be moved.
But the green frame id=13 (active, white center) and green frame id=15 (inactive, black center) are not completed. They need to be moved into dark frames.
The dark frames in the left quadrants (id=4 and id=13) are empty and have white centers. They are targets.
But the green frames can't reach the left quadrants because of the purple cross.
Wait, maybe I need to look at this from a different angle. What if the purple cross is not a permanent barrier? What if it can be removed or changed?
Actually, let me look at the purple cross more carefully. The horizontal bar is at rows 24-26, and the vertical bar is at cols 21-29. The green frame id=13 is at rows 42-47, cols 30-32. It can move UP to rows 30-35, but not further. It can move LEFT to cols 30-32, but not further.
What if I move the green frame to the corner where the horizontal and vertical bars meet? The corner is at rows 24-26, cols 21-29. The green frame is 6 rows tall and 3 cols wide. If the green frame is at rows 24-29, cols 27-29, it would overlap with both bars. But the green frame can't reach that position because it's blocked by the bars.
Actually, wait. Let me look at the purple cross boundary again:
- [26, 30] to [27, 29]: step from (26, 30) to (27, 29)
This suggests there's a gap or a step at rows 26-27, cols 29-30. Maybe the green frame can pass through this gap if it's positioned correctly.
The green frame is 6 rows tall. If the green frame is at rows 24-29, cols 30-32, it would be at the corner. The horizontal bar is at rows 24-26, and the vertical bar is at cols 21-29 (bottom part) or cols 24-62 (top part).
Actually, the purple cross is a single connected object. The horizontal bar is at rows 24-26, cols 24-62 (approximately). The vertical bar is at rows 0-62, cols 21-29 (approximately). The corner is at rows 24-26, cols 24-29.
The green frame is at rows 42-47, cols 30-32. To reach the corner, the green frame needs to move UP to rows 24-29 and LEFT to cols 27-29. But the green frame is blocked by the purple cross.
Hmm, I'm stuck again. Let me try a different approach.
Maybe the goal is not to move green frames into dark frames, but to arrange them in a specific pattern. For example, maybe the green frames need to be aligned with the dark frames in terms of row or column.
Or maybe the goal is to have the green frames "mirror" the dark frames across the purple cross. For example, the green frame id=13 is at rows 42-47, cols 30-32 in the bottom-right quadrant, and it needs to be moved to rows 42-47, cols 8-10 in the bottom-left quadrant (mirrored across the vertical bar).
But the green frame can't move LEFT past the purple bar.
Wait, maybe I need to think about this differently. What if the green frame CAN cross the purple bar, but only if it's at a specific row position? For example, maybe the green frame can cross the purple bar at rows 27-32, where there's a gap in the horizontal bar.
Let me try moving the green frame UP to rows 27-32 and then LEFT to see if it can cross the purple bar.
Actually, the green frame is at rows 42-47, cols 30-32. To move UP to rows 27-32, I need to move UP 5 times (42-47 -> 39-44 -> 36-41 -> 33-38 -> 30-35 -> 27-32).
But earlier, the green frame stopped at rows 30-35 when I tried to move UP. So the green frame can move UP to rows 30-35, but not further to rows 27-32.
Wait, the green frame is currently at rows 42-47, cols 30-32. Let me try moving UP to see how far it can go.

</function>
</tool_call>
